data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider E.ON (XTRA:EOAN) After Strong Multi Year Gains?
data.description:
• Wondering whether E.ON at €19.59 still offers value or if most of the opportunity is already priced in? This article walks through what the current share price might be telling you.
  • The stock has eased slightly in the short term, with a 0.6% decline over 7 days and a 1.6% decline over 30 days, while longer term returns sit at 19.1% year to date, 40.4% over 1 year, 88.0% over 3 years and 147.4% over 5 years.
  • Recent coverage has focused on E.ON as a core European utilities name, highlighting its role in power and gas networks and its relevance for investors tracking large listed infrastructure groups. This context helps frame the recent pullback in the share price against a longer history of strong multi year returns.
  • Despite that backdrop, E.ON currently scores 0 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's valuation checks. The sections ahead will compare different valuation approaches and then finish with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

E.ON scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: E.ON Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model looks at the value of a stock by projecting all future dividend payments and discounting them back to today. It is essentially asking what those dividend streams are worth in € right now.

For E.ON, the model uses a current dividend per share of €0.62, a return on equity of 11.10% and a payout ratio of about 77%. Future dividend growth in the model is set at 1.73%, capped from a higher starting figure, with an expected growth input of 2.55%. This combination assumes E.ON continues to pay out a large share of earnings while growing dividends at a modest pace.

Based on these inputs, the DDM arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €18.46 per share. Compared with the current share price of €19.59, that indicates the stock is about 6.1% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap and well within the sort of range income investors often tolerate for a large utility.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

E.ON is fairly valued according to our Dividend Discount Model (DDM), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for E.ON.

Approach 2: E.ON Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like E.ON, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay today directly to the earnings the business is already generating. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk typically support a higher P/E, while slower growth and higher risk usually point to a lower, more conservative range.

E.ON currently trades on a P/E of 29.54x. That stands above the Integrated Utilities industry average P/E of 19.64x and also above the peer group average of 15.37x, so the market is placing a higher earnings multiple on E.ON than on many of its closest comparables.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for E.ON is 11.69x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a “normal” P/E might look like once earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, industry, and market cap are all considered together. Because it blends these company specific factors, it can offer a more tailored reference point than simply lining E.ON up against broad industry or peer averages.

Comparing the current P/E of 29.54x with the Fair Ratio of 11.69x suggests the shares trade above the level implied by these fundamentals.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 1: Iron Mountain Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The DCF model used here projects Iron Mountain's adjusted funds from operations into the future and discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the business could be worth in dollars right now.

Iron Mountain's latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.54b. Based on analyst inputs for the next few years, and then Simply Wall St's extrapolations beyond that, free cash flow is projected to reach $2.49b by 2030. Over the 2026 to 2035 period, the model uses a two stage approach, with earlier cash flows guided by analyst estimates and later years based on calculated growth assumptions.

Combining all of those discounted cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $158.10 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $109.37, this implies that Iron Mountain trades at a 30.8% discount to this DCF estimate, which indicates that the stock currently screens as undervalued on this model.

This is especially relevant for REITs and service heavy models where earnings can be affected by non cash items.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/S ratio partly reflects what investors expect for future growth and how much risk they see in those cash flows. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while lower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower one.

data.title: Eurobio Scientific (ENXTPA:ALERS) Margin Improvement To 2.9% Tests Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme (ENXTPA:ALERS) has reported FY 2025 first half revenue of €80.8 million and basic EPS of €0.23, setting the tone for a year where margins and earnings quality are firmly in focus for investors. The company has seen revenue move from €73.3 million in the first half of 2024 to €81.5 million in the second half of 2024 and then to €80.8 million in the latest period, while basic EPS shifted from €0.40 to effectively flat and now €0.23, against a backdrop of a 2.9% net profit margin over the last 12 months that frames a tighter, margin-driven story rather than a pure topline one.

See our full analysis for Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the most widely held narratives around Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme and where the fresh data challenges those stories.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

2.9% net margin points to tight profitability

  • Over the last 12 months, Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme converted €169 million of revenue into €4.9 million of net income, which works out to a 2.9% net margin compared with 2.6% a year earlier.
  • What challenges the bearish worry about a collapsing business is that, despite a 61% per year earnings decline over five years, the most recent year showed 22.6% earnings growth versus that five year average, while recent half year revenues held in a tight band between €73.3 million and €81.5 million.
    • Skeptics highlight the long run earnings decline, yet the trailing 12 month margin and €4.9 million net income sit above the older five year average earnings level.
    • Bears also focus on earnings volatility, but the last three reported halves show revenue in the €73 million to €81.5 million range, which gives some underpinning to that recent margin improvement.

Multi year earnings slide meets richer 47.9x P/E

  • The shares trade on a 47.9x trailing P/E, well above both the European Biotechs industry at 15.2x and a peer group at 14.7x, while reported earnings have declined by 61% per year over the past five years.
  • Bears argue that such a rich multiple is hard to square with the long run earnings slide, and the numbers give that concern some weight because the 47.9x P/E is over 3x the sector and peer averages even after that 61% annual decline in earnings.
    • Critics highlight that paying more than 3x the industry P/E exposes holders if earnings were to revert closer to the weaker multi year trend.
    • What complicates the bearish call is that the last 12 months delivered 22.6% earnings growth versus the five year average, so the P/E is being set against a year that is better than the longer history.

DCF fair value of €52.98 vs €23.30 price

  • A DCF fair value of €52.98 compared with a share price of €23.30 suggests the stock trades about 56% below that cash flow based estimate, even as the trailing 12 month net margin sits at 2.9%.
  • Supporters of a more bullish stance point out that this DCF gap heavily supports the idea of mispricing, yet the high 47.9x P/E and the 61% per year five year earnings decline mean investors still need to reconcile a model implying upside with market metrics that price in a lot of past volatility.
    • What is striking is that the DCF fair value is more than double the share price, while the bottom line over the last year improved versus the five year average, which some see as early confirmation of that model.
    • On the other hand, the same data set shows only a 2.9% net margin and a long run slide in earnings, so any bullish case has to lean on the DCF assumptions rather than a long track record of high profitability.

To see how other investors are weighing these trade offs and how the numbers fit into a bigger story, check out the latest community narrative for Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Eurobio Scientific Société anonyme.

data.title: Al Ramz Real Estate's (TADAWUL:4327) Earnings Are Weaker Than They Seem
data.description:
Despite announcing strong earnings, Al Ramz Real Estate Company's (TADAWUL:4327) stock was sluggish. We think that the market might be paying attention to some underlying factors that they find to be concerning.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against Al Ramz Real Estate's Earnings

As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

For the year to December 2025, Al Ramz Real Estate had an accrual ratio of 0.72. As a general rule, that bodes poorly for future profitability. And indeed, during the period the company didn't produce any free cash flow whatsoever. Over the last year it actually had negative free cash flow of ر.س760m, in contrast to the aforementioned profit of ر.س281.0m. We also note that Al Ramz Real Estate's free cash flow was actually negative last year as well, so we could understand if shareholders were bothered by its outflow of ر.س760m.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Al Ramz Real Estate.

Our Take On Al Ramz Real Estate's Profit Performance

As we discussed above, we think Al Ramz Real Estate's earnings were not supported by free cash flow, which might concern some investors. As a result, we think it may well be the case that Al Ramz Real Estate's underlying earnings power is lower than its statutory profit. But the good news is that its EPS growth over the last three years has been very impressive. The goal of this article has been to assess how well we can rely on the statutory earnings to reflect the company's potential, but there is plenty more to consider. data.title: ASP Isotopes (ASPI) Revenue Surge Tests Bullish Growth Narratives Against Deepening Losses
data.description:
ASP Isotopes (ASPI) has just put out its FY 2025 numbers, with Q4 revenue of about US$16.7 million and a Q4 basic EPS loss of US$0.76. Trailing twelve month revenue sits at roughly US$23.8 million against a TTM basic EPS loss of US$2.11. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from around US$1.2 million in Q4 FY 2024 to US$16.7 million in Q4 FY 2025, while trailing twelve month revenue shifted from about US$4.1 million to US$23.8 million over the same window. Investors will now be weighing those top line gains against a TTM net loss of US$175.1 million as they assess how quickly margins can tighten.

See our full analysis for ASP Isotopes.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to compare these results with the widely held growth and risk narratives around ASP Isotopes to see which storylines hold up and which start to look stretched.

Revenue jumps to US$23.8 million on a trailing basis

  • On a trailing twelve month view, revenue sits at US$23.8 million, compared with US$4.1 million for the TTM period ending Q4 FY 2024, while TTM net income shows a loss of US$175.1 million versus a loss of US$35.1 million a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the more bullish investors is the combination of this higher TTM revenue with forecasts for around 49.4% annual revenue growth and earnings projected to rise about 67.11% per year, which they see as consistent with a business that is starting to scale, even though the current TTM loss of US$175.1 million highlights that the company is still a long way from the positive earnings those forecasts point to.
    • Bulls often highlight that quarterly revenue moved from roughly US$1.2 million in Q4 FY 2024 to US$16.7 million in Q4 FY 2025. They pair this shift with the 49.4% revenue growth forecast as evidence that reported sales are starting to reflect the growth story.
    • At the same time, the shift in TTM basic EPS from a loss of US$0.63 to a loss of US$2.11 challenges any simple bullish view, because it shows that, so far, the growing revenue base has not yet translated into smaller losses.

Losses widen to US$175.1 million TTM

  • Trailing twelve month net income shows a loss of US$175.1 million, compared with a TTM loss of US$35.1 million one year earlier, while quarterly net income for Q2 and Q4 FY 2025 alone accounts for US$75.1 million and US$78.7 million of that loss.
  • Critics who take a more bearish view point to this pattern of widening losses, together with the comment that losses have grown at about 80.3% per year over the last five years, as a sign that the business has not yet shown clear progress toward profitability despite higher revenue. They also highlight the substantial shareholder dilution over the past year as a concrete cost of funding those losses.
    • The quarterly figures show that the two largest losses in FY 2025, US$75.1 million in Q2 and US$78.7 million in Q4, occurred in the same year that TTM revenue reached US$23.8 million. Bears see this as evidence that higher sales have come alongside, not instead of, heavy spending.
    • Given that the company remains unprofitable and has seen losses expand over several years, bearish investors focus on how much additional capital might be required if forecasted earnings improvements do not arrive on the timeline implied by the projected move to profitability within three years.

Rich 23.7x P/S multiple raises the bar

  • The shares trade on a P/S of 23.7x, compared with a P/S of 1.1x for the broader US Chemicals industry and 1.0x for peers, while the current share price is US$4.48.
  • What is striking for valuation focused investors is that this high P/S sits next to both the TTM loss of US$175.1 million and forecasts that already assume strong progress, with expectations for revenue to grow about 49.4% per year and earnings to become positive within three years. Any shortfall relative to those forecasts could weigh more heavily when sentiment is already paying such a premium multiple.
    • Supporters of the growth case point to the projected 67.11% annual earnings growth and the shift in quarterly revenue from around US$1.1 million to US$16.7 million as reasons they are willing to accept a 23.7x P/S, even though the company currently reports basic EPS losses each quarter.
    • On the other side, more cautious investors combine the high P/S with the history of shareholder dilution and above market share price volatility over the past three months. They view that combination as a sign that the current valuation already bakes in a lot of the growth story before profitability is visible in the reported numbers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Graco Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes projected future cash flows and then discounts them back to today using a required return to estimate what the business might be worth per share right now.

For Graco, the model uses last twelve month free cash flow of about $568.1 million as a starting point. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates out to 2028, with Simply Wall St extending these projections further using its own assumptions. By 2035, the ten year framework includes forecast free cash flows that reach just over $1.0b, all still expressed in $ and then discounted back to today.

On this basis, the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $94.58 per share. Compared with the current share price of $87.61, this implies the stock trades at roughly a 7.4% discount to the model’s estimate. This result sits in a fairly tight range rather than pointing to an extreme mispricing.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Graco is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Graco.

Approach 2: Graco Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings, which makes it a common cross check alongside a DCF model.

In simple terms, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk tend to justify a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower, more conservative multiple. That context matters when you compare any single P/E figure to the market.

Graco currently trades on a P/E of 27.83x, very close to both the Machinery industry average of 27.89x and the peer average of 27.42x. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 21.39x, which is the P/E you might expect for Graco given factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for the company’s own fundamentals rather than assuming all Machinery stocks deserve the same multiple. Since Graco’s actual P/E of 27.83x sits meaningfully above the Fair Ratio of 21.39x, the shares appear more expensive than that model would suggest.

Result: OVERVALUED
